tldr: Headlines across 10 countries lead overwhelmingly with the US troop-review angle, but diverge sharply in tone: most outlets report a security policy shift while tabloids and state media amplify Hegseth's confrontational rhetoric; Mexico's La Jornada uniquely foregrounds financial coercion; Italy's ANSA alone reads the event as cooperative; and the US Daily Wire pivots entirely to Iran.

frames.sub_frame: US forces under review in Europe

evidence.quote: review of U.S. troops deployed to NATO
US to review forces in Europe
US Will Review Force Presence in Europe
US to withdraw forces from Europe
US will review presence in Europe
review of US troops in Europe, scorns some allies
Trump aumenta la presión sobre los aliados
US secretary of war blasts 'shameful' NATO

frames.sub_frame: US threatens to withhold NATO dues

evidence.quote: Amenaza EU con retener una parte de sus cuotas

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to pivot entirely away from the NATO troop review, leading with Iran — 'Hegseth's Deal-Or-Consequences Message To Iran' — recasting the Brussels speech as a US military deterrence signal toward Tehran rather than an alliance pressure event.
Only outlet to frame the troop reduction as cooperative ('US agreeing on path with allies'), softening what the other 13 headlines present as a unilateral ultimatum or confrontation.

Only outlet to frame the troop review as a potential NATO exit at headline level — 'is this the beginning of Trump’s pulling the plug on NATO?' — treating it as possibly irreversible rather than a bargaining tactic.

Only outlet to lead with the financial coercion angle ('threatens to withhold dues to NATO'), shifting the story from military force levels to economic leverage — a framing absent from every English-language headline.

The word 'again' in 'Hegseth scolds NATO allies — again' editorially normalises the confrontation as a recurring pattern, framing it as unremarkable escalation where other outlets treat it as a significant policy shift.

Only outlet to foreground Hegseth's word 'shameful' in the headline — amplifying NATO-fracture rhetoric without editorial context, in direct service of Moscow's interest in deepening transatlantic discord.

bottom_line: The headline layer presents Hegseth's Brussels speech as a military-security ultimatum — US forces under review, Europe told to self-defend — with financial coercion surfacing only in Mexico's left press and the Iran angle surfacing only in US right-wing media.
